speaker: interviewer
text: Fania Wedro. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to our town, Korzec, I came for the-- you know, again, you know, I was working, you know, in the town. I was going back and forth, wherever they sent me. Then it was said whoever was a Polish citizen before the war can go back into Poland. I was raised with-- with-- with-- with Stalin, with-- with all that. What I know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't know the difference, where I was going, what. But everyone in town left. In the meantime, my sister-in-law already left, you know, with the baby. So I was from the last one. So I figured out, well, everyone is going. What am I going stay here, you know? So I went too. Where I was going, I swear to you I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they came into the-- you know, I went with a-- with a truck, and then with a train. That's all I remember, going on it, you know. And we came into-- it-- it took us a long time, quite a while, you know, to go over the-- the-- they-- they checked. They didn't even check passports. Whatever. They-- they-- they just-- I walked through. To some they checked. Me they never checked. And we came into a place [NON-ENGLISH]. We came to [NON-ENGLISH]. They said everybody was going-- it was like [NON-ENGLISH], you know, anyway, in a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that? Where was [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] was Poland. It's not far from Katowice there. So when we-- I came in there, there were names on the walls, written. I mean, and it was-- they had a register, and also little notes. This one is looking for this one. And this one is looking for this one. And this one-- I went and put my name in the register. And I said, I'm looking here for a family [? Edelmann. ?] A lady stayed beside me, young girl. She says, I know, they live on [? Krakowski ?] 28. So she took me there the same day, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was-- which family was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? Edelmann. ?] It's my-- my sister-in-law. But, of course, they lived in such small quarters, and everything. So I came down there. And right away, the next day, I registered. And I went [NON-ENGLISH] for single people and so on. There's a kibbutz which you can gather. So I came in, and I joined. Excuse me. I joined the kibbutz [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was-- listen, we had food. We had-- they gave me some clothes, and so on. And you know, with [NON-ENGLISH], it's mine is yours, and yours is mine, and mine is yours. It's a-- it's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a collective.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a collective, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that for the purpose of-- of making [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] to Israel, you know, that was the purpose. And that was my purpose. All of a sudden, I already-- I hear a Hebrew word, and I see Hebrew, and I see Jewish, and it was nice. They were speaking, down there, Polish, and I was so happy. I used to make-- I was making out already the slogans, because I remembered that from-- from school, that I learned at school. This [NON-ENGLISH] whatever. It's a-- so it was-- it was-- it was really, to me, it opened up a new world, and it reminded me about the school. I always had a will to-- to-- to learn. So they sent me-- the kibbutz sent me to a seminar to learn how to be a leader from another-- you know, when new children are coming in. So I was already-- no more, I was already a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was real.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1945. When we came-- then, it was time to make [NON-ENGLISH]. We're going to [NON-ENGLISH]. How are we going? Over the mountains, the Carpathian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do I have? A little parcel. The parcel wasn't bigger than this box. That was my belongings.

speaker: interviewee
text: We get over Carpathian, you know. A night before on that road, 20 from [NON-ENGLISH] were killed, because going over the border. Us, thank god, we went over. But we went. At the top, that little parcel which I had also fell down. And then I lost even that. And we went into a-- they took us into a barn. We stayed down there. I think we stayed a couple of days. And during the night they came and they took us again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- I think two people came. They must have been from the [NON-ENGLISH], you know. They came, and they-- they told us we have to be quiet and quiet, so on. And we-- we walked. We walked a whole night. And also again into another barn, another place. Then they said we'll be-- we'll walk, and we'll come to a train. But we have to speak Hebrew, because we are Greeks. So we went as Greeks. So not everybody could speak Hebrew. This one says, [HEBREW]. The other one says [HEBREW]. You know, it's-- they were quoting from the Bible, you know, from the prayer books. And then we came in to Vienna. Vienna there was already-- I saw a city. I saw it's nice, you know. I mean it's-- it was already-- I didn't even know that such a thing exists, except what I saw in pictures. And we were in Vienna very short time. It was a-- just a transit. And from Vienna, they-- they took us again with-- with-- with-- with trucks, you know. And we came in to-- actually, it was-- I came into [NON-ENGLISH], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria. It's all happened in Austria. And as I was down there, his father came in. I was staying at a family. And he said, whatever you doing in here? Come to our place Bad Gastein. But, you know, it was very hard to go from one camp to another, because you-- you had to be registered. And somehow they registered.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a camp was that? What it--

speaker: interviewee
text: A DP camp. So when I came in to Bad Gastein, they were staying in hotels. There were five hotels. There were the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH], and so on and so forth. Israeli teachers-- Israeli teachers came from a seminar to start to-- you know. So I registered, whoever speaks Hebrew. And you know, I spoke a very good Hebrew. Well, now I don't use the language, you know. I spoke a very good. That was my language. So they-- they read. We-- we had a seminar. And then we were able to teach.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you a teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was teaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which grades did you teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grade 1. I till now remembered, I had two beautiful student, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]. [? Janek ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did-- how long did you teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I teached for a couple years. And then it came in and some of them-- you know, we had the United Nations. Some of them were taller than me. They were 15, 16-year-olds, weren't in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was, can you imagine, for Hebrew, their-- their teacher. They were so tall. So one time I-- I have to be [? somewheres ?] [? on a ?] newsreel because General Eisenhower was supposed to come. So he couldn't make it, so General Clark came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they couldn't believe it. Here sits a short one, a chubby one, you know, short and chubby and [? dark ?] girl, and she teaches such big, big boys she teaches. So they couldn't get over it, you know. And they took the pictures and so on. But-- but I earned. I earned a living. I-- I didn't take from no one. And then we-- we-- then I had private-- I tutored private lessons. I used to give private lessons in Hebrew. And, you know, it wasn't very bad. It was quite nice, you know. I mean, it just that we didn't have a permanent home. We didn't have a home. We didn't have a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay? What kind of-- kind of accommodations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The accommodations were fine. I mean, I would stay with two other people in one room. I mean, the accommodations weren't big. They're five, six people in a room. But it was a hotel, years ago, Austria. Hotel Austria. And we stayed down there for a while. And then they transferred, they eliminated that camp, and they transferred us to Eblesburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was also in Austria. We were under the American zone. And we were-- and I was teaching there too, you know. And I had very good students. And I enjoyed it. I really enjoyed the kids. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grade 1. Sometimes I subbed for grade 2 when the teacher was sick. So he says, I wouldn't take no one else, I'll take-- I'll take Fania to-- to-- to this, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- it was a very-- it was quite an experience, you know. But just the same, I was alone. I had no one. Especially when I came to camp, but it came a holiday, I was really-- everybody had-- this one had a cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had someone, a distant relative. I had no one. And it's such a awful feeling to be all alone in the world that some things you just-- you're like-- you want to live. You want to survive. You-- you want everything, but sometimes your life isn't worth anything when you look around and there's no one special [NON-ENGLISH]. It's-- it's-- it's terrible. It's a awful feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next I-- we-- so we were in the-- I met my husband. His father married us. Married us. Married off his son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what's his name? Your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo, you know. So then when-- when I-- I married Leo, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married in 1948. In '47, we had the-- the, you know, civil-- because you needed, down there, in Austria, a German, and then '48 I had the-- the real wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jewish, if you can call-- I mean, we can call it a wedding, anyway. We were married. And then we were going to go to Israel, because I-- Israel was in my heart, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened, his father-- they-- those days, they didn't take older people. And he had his father, so they wouldn't take-- his people to-- you know, to-- because it was [NON-ENGLISH], was illegal. And he wasn't that strong, had high blood pressure.

speaker: interviewee
text: The meantime, while I was sitting down there, in the-- in-- in Bad Gastein and in [NON-ENGLISH], he-- he found-- he knew he had a brother in Canada. So he got with the brother. And the brother sent him out papers, although he didn't meet his brother. His brother died before he came. Didn't see him for 40 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came to Canada, but those days you could take out only farmers out. You see, and farm-- and single people. So he went as a single.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Winnipeg, they were going to send me out. My father-in-law's friend was sending me out papers. They did send out papers, but they got lost, the papers, somewheres in the shuffle. So I came as a domestic. I signed up as a domestic, and I was coming to work the contract in order to come to Canada--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive here?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to come to be with my husband. And I arrived in 1948, December. Bitter cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go? To which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edmonton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why Edmonton?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Edmonton. We-- we arrived-- because that where my husband was. So I came to Halifax, and I says, now you can send me back, but I says, I've got a husband. So what? They-- they'll never-- they didn't send me back, because, you see, I-- I had a mix up with papers in-- in-- they mixed me up in Italy. My name was [? Elman ?] and another girl name was [? Telman. ?] So they-- they'd switched photographs down there in the office. So they left us in Italy. So I registered in Italy as-- again, as a domestic, but those days, they did-- you know, as I figured out, I'll come as a non-Jew. I'll have a better chance as a domestic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sure enough, I registered as a non-Jew. So when I came to Halifax, I was met by the priest, you know. So and then we-- I-- I forget. St. Paul, I think it was, a place that we-- we stayed down there for about two, three weeks till they'd know what to do with me. So in the meantime, I worked in the hotel for them there until they would know what they'll do with me. And they said they decided they're going to send me-- it's OK to let me in. I didn't hijack a plane. So maybe if I would have hijacked, I would have had difficulties coming in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was all right for me to come. So I came to Edmonton. It was bitter cold. Bitter cold in 1948, in December. And that's how I arrived into-- to Edmonton, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do-- what was your occupation since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since then, I plucked chickens, because I didn't have a language. And I didn't have the language. And I worked part time as a teacher's helper in a kindergarten. Of course, I didn't have the papers, what was-- you know, the-- what's here required.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- in the mean time, in 1949, a child was born. I had a child in December. I mean in September, and it was three months sick. And it was in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it wasn't like nowadays. It was Alberta Health Care or whatever, and doctors. You know, you have Medicare. Those days, a visit from a doctor was $5. And when you earn only $18 a week, you can't-- and in the hospital, we had to pay. So I went, and I worked. I went to the hospital. I was offered by organizations that help. I said no, so I went, and I was plucking chickens. And we went and made arrangements with the hospital, and I paid them out at $2 and $5 a month, and paid them out. And I paid up the doctor bills. We paid up the hospital bills. Paid everyone off. So I was plucking chickens. I had-- then we bought-- this one-- I was putting up pickles, and I was selling-- selling them to the butcher wholesale. And then, finally, we bought our first house. We rented out every room. I had boarders, you know. And I went to work. But that time, I started to work already in a department store, for Mr. [? Borsden. ?] He gave me a chance. I came to him, and I says, now, look. Hire me. If I'll be good, it's fine. If not, don't hire me. So he hired me. And I did well. I managed the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from odd jobs you ended up having--

speaker: interviewee
text: From odd jobs we went into grocery business. And then we went into [INAUDIBLE] in a prestigious building. We had the Wedro's a-- a business. And then we went into hotel business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Banff. It's-- it was a long journey up, believe me. But we-- we climbed up to the ladder, and we raised a very nice family, my son, my-- my daughter. They're-- they're-- I'm proud of them. And thank god, you know. We-- we-- we-- we built a life, a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is in Wisconsin, and my daughter is in Florida. And I have a wonderful granddaughter. She's the apple of our eye. And, you know, we-- we are survivors, you know. The road isn't easy. Sometimes it's hard, and so on. But you know what? How many times we fall, that time we get up, and we keep on going, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your hometown since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '92, they put up a [NON-ENGLISH]. The town got together for my husband's town. And I went. That was for 3,000 people. And in '93-- in-- in '93, they did in our town. We got together and put-- no. This-- then we got together, but we didn't put it up yet, because when I was in [NON-ENGLISH], you know, we put up-- they took me to [NON-ENGLISH], and down there were five graves. The Ukrainian put up a [NON-ENGLISH]. One.

speaker: interviewer
text: A memorial.

speaker: interviewee
text: A memorial. Yes, they did put up. But then he tells me-- deep in the woods. He took me by jeep down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very hard to get in there. And the-- the-- the-- the head of the-- said, there's five more, these little ones. So he says, what's the difference between them and them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I came home, I says, no, there's no way. My father is in there, and we have to put a [NON-ENGLISH] for all of them together. So we went, and myself and my husband, with difficulties from afar, and we did put up a [NON-ENGLISH]. And in-- that was-- and in '94, we put up in our town. I mean, in-- for our city, for the 5,000 people. This is that collective, in [NON-ENGLISH]. I-- we put it in ourselves. And now--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a memorial did you put up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's a beautiful with-- we put up all around with-- with Magen Davids and the fence, Magen Davids and [NON-ENGLISH]. And with-- with a gate to go in. And they gave us-- we brought earth from Israel, and we put it inside to-- to join it together. And we brought-- we-- we had such a big [NON-ENGLISH], they had the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Memorial service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, had big one. They came down. They gave us-- the-- the government came, and so on, and we went. We had-- we brought flax from Israel. We brought flax from Israel, and so on. It was-- [NON-ENGLISH] was sung, and so it was-- it was beautiful. I still have the videos from it. It was-- it was something. You know, I felt that I-- as I'm alive, I have an obligation to do that for my parents, you know. And now I went again for [NON-ENGLISH] at least. If I know where they are, and if I wouldn't do that, that would be the biggest crime in the world. I must have been left for a purpose, you know. And now, what we did, there were grazing-- cattles were grazing on the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: On the cemeteries.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the cemetery. So what we did, we-- now it's over 800 feet of [NON-ENGLISH] fence. We fenced in our town. So that what I went for this [INAUDIBLE] occasion. We fenced it all in, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I know where my grandmother is. They are in peace. There won't be grazing. Cattle won't be grazing in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Ukrainian government is very helpful. Very, very helpful. I mean, to look after-- to look after it, we have to do ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I look-- I undertook the costs for Kazak, which is the three graves for the 5,000 people our town, and where my father is, for the 350 people. This is my obligation. And I-- we send in the monies to look after that, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's-- when I come-- came from there, I had a very good feeling. I feel-- but, I feel like I have to go back. It's part of me is still there. It's part of me. I come down there. I feel I talk to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about the people there when you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: You talk to them, everyone left after-- everybody came after '45. Nobody was there before '45. But I met the guy that took my father. He died.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says-- I said, do you know who I am? I talked, and he says, eh, no. He [? is ?] [? then. ?] You know, I'm-- I'm [? Pavlovsky. ?] And I says, which [? Pavlovsky, ?] [? Surrar? ?] Yes. And such-- such guy. He says, and I live in this and this house, in [? Linux ?] house. And I just looked at him. And then I bent down, and all of a sudden, he said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. I says, I was a dirty Jew for you. All of a sudden [NON-ENGLISH]? He got up and he left. So when I told that to the-- to the head, you know, to the mayor of the city, actually from the province, you see, I went and I spoke to him. He says, you know what? He said, if we would have had to put in jail, we had to put 75% people away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I came he's dead. He died. Because, you see, you come to [NON-ENGLISH], you've got a street [NON-ENGLISH], a street [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a-- a very-- which was a very [NON-ENGLISH]. I mean, there are known people which they're such a-- they killed us. They murdered us. The pogrom and they did. [NON-ENGLISH]. And they've got now streets to them. They're heroes, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fania, now it's over 50 years after the war. Is there anything you want to share with your family, with future generations? Any lessons learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I would like to share that they shouldn't forgotten. A thing like that should have never happened, but it did happen. And it can happen, if we don't-- we are not careful. I want that respect should be paid for our departed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can tell you one thing, when they says, what in the world are you going down there? It's dangerous. What in the world are you going there? That radiation. I says, if it's not hard for them to lay under the ground, shouldn't be hard for us to walk to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want my children, the future generation, to come to [NON-ENGLISH] and pay the respect that they deserve, because they actually fought us out to freedom. Because if we wouldn't have had a Holocaust, nobody-- we wouldn't have had not a state. We wouldn't have had with our heads going up so high to be a Jew. And we-- they paid a price. And we paid a very big price. And I think it shouldn't be forgotten. And it should be reminded that such a thing could have never happened. We should have to talk. As much as it's hard to talk about it, we have to talk. And we have to tell. Cause like [NON-ENGLISH] did with his [NON-ENGLISH], it was [NON-ENGLISH]. Everything went by word of mouth. And that's my message to my children. Remember your grandparents. Remember that if you ever have time, go to [NON-ENGLISH]. They deserve it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, camera's ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my husband, Leo. And we're married 48 years. It's going to be in February 28, 49.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 49. We're lucky, because it-- it-- it had 40-- 29 days. We were married on 28. So at least I've got anniversary. And I expect a present. And this I can tell you, one thing. We-- we raised a family, a beautiful family together. We went-- it was a hard-- a hard climb, hard to heal, you know, but we made it to the top. And hope we don't go downhill. I hope we stay at the top for a long, long time. And [NON-ENGLISH], amen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in this picture is my father, my mother, myself, and my brother. My brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the baby down there. I was two years old. And next to me is my father, Yossef, and my mother, Chaya, and my brother, [? Shmuel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken 57 years-- 67 years ago. And I-- I'm-- I'm two years old in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Korzec. In Korzec, at home. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was in-- my father had a sister in Argentina. And after the war, she sent me the picture. And I restored it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sent the picture before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, it went to Argentina. So that how we-- you see, that's the only picture I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at the necklace. The hairdo. Cross eyed. My father was a handsome man. Look how cross eyed I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're looking to the side. Photographer was probably--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no I had-- I-- I was cross eyed. You can go and get your medicine, Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're ready. Fania, what certificate is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a teaching-- teaching certificate. It was given out to me in 1947 for a job well done. I was recommended for [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was teaching Hebrew. I was teaching grade 1 in the DP camps. I was teaching school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which DP camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bad Gastein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. What-- what is this certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my marriage certificate. It was taken in Lienz, October 11th, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Lienz? Which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in Austria. You see, this is the-- I write-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know. I can't even pronounce it properly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- well it wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were at the DP camp, but this is-- this was taken in Lienz, in the-- in the city hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a document which I was in a-- in camp in Italy. It was a transient camp, so when I had to leave from one place to another, so they had to give me a-- a-- a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: A permit.

speaker: interviewee
text: A permit. And that's the permit what was given to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your only form of identification at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1948. In September, in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What's this certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a ID certificate by the-- by-- by the [NON-ENGLISH] of a teacher's certificate. A Hebrew-- a Hebrew certificate for in the camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad Gastein, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, so you're saying this is a [NON-ENGLISH]. What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a handwritten [NON-ENGLISH], taken in 19-- in 1948, February the 28. And it's written in-- by the rabbi's home in Lienz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
